narration: Here's a weird money trick your brain pulls on you every single time. It puts your money into invisible buckets — and those buckets are costing you over six thousand dollars a year.
visual: Dark background. A large piggy bank SVG center-screen. Three glowing labeled buckets appear around it: BONUS, PAYCHECK, GIFT. Each bucket fills with animated coin drops at different speeds.

narration: Nobel Prize winner Richard Thaler called it mental accounting. The rule says all money is equal — a dollar is a dollar. But your brain completely ignores that rule.
visual: Light background. Person silhouette with thought bubble containing two dollar bills — one labeled BONUS glowing amber, one labeled PAYCHECK in gray. An equals sign between them gets crossed out.

narration: Number one: you spend windfall money — bonuses, tax refunds, gifts — three times faster than your regular paycheck. It feels like found money, so your brain gives it a free pass.
visual: Dark background. Split screen: left shows paycheck with slow-draining bar; right shows bonus check with rapidly emptying bar labeled 3x FASTER. Animated dollar signs fly out of the right bar.

narration: Number two: only twelve percent of bonuses ever get saved, versus thirty-two percent of equivalent regular income. Same money, completely different fate — just because of where it came from.
visual: Light background. Two bar charts side by side: BONUS SAVED 12% (short amber bar) vs PAYCHECK SAVED 32% (taller green bar). Percentage numbers count up. A house icon sits above the green bar.

narration: Number three: seventy-three percent of people have a secret fun money bucket for gift cards and cash gifts — and they blow it guilt-free on stuff they'd never buy with a paycheck.
visual: Light background. A gift card and cash bills float into a glowing bucket labeled FUN MONEY — NO RULES. A guilt-o-meter shows zero. Then credit card and shopping bag appear with red X through the paycheck icon.

narration: The fix is dead simple: when a windfall hits, move it to your main account and wait seventy-two hours. Your brain stops treating it as free money — and you keep the six thousand dollars.
visual: Dark background. Bonus check arrow pointing into bank account icon. A 72-hour countdown timer ticks down. Piggy bank fills up glowing amber with $6,200 SAVED text appearing. Follow for more money psychology.
